one_line_takeaway: Mantis weaponizes LLM agents' own susceptibility to prompt injection as a proactive cyber-defense, using hidden-payload decoys to either hack back the attacker's machine or trap its agent in a resource-draining tarpit, achieving ~95% sabotage success and <3% attacker success against three open-source pentest agents on easy CTFs.
key_finding: Across all configurations Mantis achieved roughly a 95.4% success rate at its sabotage objective (objD) while cutting the attacker's flag-capture success (objA) to under 3%, versus a No-Defense baseline where the strongest agent, PentestGPTAuto, captured the flag about 9-10 out of 10 times. Active defense (agent-counterstrike) was the most reliable because it needs the fewest attacker actions (once the reverse shell opens, the objective is met), and the FTP decoy outperformed the Web-app decoy (anonymous FTP login is a simpler, more attractive exploit than crafting a working SQL injection). The main failure mode: when a decoy exploit fails repeatedly the agent redirects to a real service (e.g., the genuine SMB server on CTF:Dancing) and never receives the injection, making CTF:Dancing with the Web-app decoy the weakest case. For passive defense, escalating tarpit directory complexity drove GPT-4o inference cost up materially (reported per-attack API costs of $0.81, $1.6, and $4.55 across the three agents/complexity settings, with history-replaying PentestGPTAuto the most expensive to run). A key qualitative observation: once inside the tarpit, agents never backtracked or abandoned the futile depth-first search, unlike a human.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Mantis (Malicious LLM-Agent Neutralization and exploitation Through prompt Injections), a defensive framework that flips prompt injection from a vulnerability into a defense against autonomous LLM-driven cyberattacks. Mantis deploys intentionally vulnerable decoy services (FTP, and a Web-app with a SQL-injectable login) to lure an attacking LLM-agent, then, once the agent exploits the decoy, an 'injection manager' plants a hidden prompt injection into the service response. Two opposite 'sabotage objectives' are supported: (1) active defense / agent-counterstrike, which manipulates the attacker's agent into opening a reverse shell back on the attacker's own machine (hack-back), and (2) passive defense / agent-tarpit, which traps the agent in an infinite fake FTP filesystem to stall it and inflate its inference cost. A distinguishing engineering element is that the injected payloads are hidden from human operators using ANSI escape sequences (the conceal operator \033[8m) and HTML comment tags, so a person viewing the decoy in a terminal or browser does not see them while the LLM still ingests the raw text. NOTE ON CONTRIBUTION BOUNDARY: the attacker agents (PentestGPT, AutoPenAgent/AutoPenBench, HackingBuddyGPT, AutoAttacker, PenHeal) are prior work the authors use as adversaries to test against, NOT contributions of this paper; the authors' own additions on the attacker side are limited to 'PentestGPTAuto,' a wrapper (a 'weaponizer' LLM module) they built to make the non-autonomous PentestGPT run end-to-end, and adding Claude backend support to some agents.
threat_model: A two-party game between an attacker A (an autonomous LLM-agent running the full cyber kill chain, knowing only the target's IP) and a defender D operating on target machine S. The defender is deliberately weak/agnostic: unaware of the LLM the attacker uses, unaware of its objective, and unaware of the actual exploitable vulnerability in S (so it cannot patch it beforehand). The defender's only action is deploying Mantis. Attacker wins by capturing the CTF flag within a capped number of rounds (30); defender wins only if the attacker fails to capture the flag AND the defender achieves its predefined sabotage objective objD (a working reverse shell for counterstrike, or sustaining max rounds inside the tarpit). Note this is an unusual, offense-inverting threat model: the 'defense' involves the defender actively inducing code execution on the attacker's host, which the authors flag raises legal/ethical hack-back concerns (all experiments confined to isolated VMs / HackTheBox sandboxes).
limitations: (1) Evaluated only on three beginner-level CTF machines; the authors argue harder CTFs would confound defense success with the agent's own inability to exploit, and their Appendix A shows agents scored 0% on 'Chemistry'/'Cicada' even without any defense, so real-world efficacy on capable attackers is untested. (2) The defense is inherently adaptive-adversary-fragile: the authors concede that once attackers know Mantis is deployed they can instruct agents to skip decoys or filter out the fixed pool of execution triggers, reintroducing a human-in-the-loop. (3) Only a single decoy and single objective per run were tested. (4) Small statistical base (10 runs per cell). (5) The active-defense (hack-back) mode carries unresolved legal/ethical liability and is defeated by a well-sandboxed attacker host, which the authors acknowledge. (6) Concealment relies on ANSI/HTML tricks that a raw-text-inspecting attacker (or a defensively prompted agent) could strip.

datasets_used: HackTheBox 'very-easy' CTF machines: CTF:Dancing (Windows, SMB with improper auth), CTF:Redeemer (Linux, misconfigured Redis), CTF:Synced (Linux, anonymous RSYNC)
HackTheBox advanced CTFs used only in Appendix A: 'Chemistry' and 'Cicada'
Attacker agents (prior work, used as adversaries): PentestGPTAuto (authors' autonomized PentestGPT), AutoPenAgent, HackingBuddyGPT
Backend LLMs: GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (main); GPT-4 and Claude 3.5 Haiku (appendix)
quantitative_results: Overall Mantis sabotage-objective (objD) success rate ~95.4% across all configurations
Attacker flag-capture (objA) success reduced to under 3% when Mantis is deployed
No-Defense baseline: PentestGPTAuto captured the flag ~9-10/10 across the three CTFs (e.g., 9/10 Dancing, 9/10 Redeemer, 10/10 Synced with GPT-4o)
agent-counterstrike + FTP decoy against PentestGPTAuto: objA 0/10 and objD 10/10 on Dancing, Redeemer, and Synced (GPT-4o)
Weakest cell: agent-counterstrike + Web-app decoy on CTF:Dancing, where attacker occasionally won (e.g., objA 1/10, objD 9/10)
Round cap set to 30; average rounds to compromise a machine with no defense ~5.6; with Mantis, sabotage typically completes in ~4-5 rounds

Tarpit cost-draining on GPT-4o (first 10 tarpit rounds, CTF:Dancing, FTP decoy): reported per-attack API costs of $0.81, $1.6, and $4.55 as subdirectories-per-node scale (5/50/150), with PentestGPTAuto most expensive due to history replay and HackingBuddyGPT cheapest

Appendix A: on advanced CTFs Chemistry and Cicada, PentestGPTAuto+GPT-4o scored 0% flag capture even without Mantis (5 trials each); with Mantis active (agent-counterstrike + FTP) it reported 100% misdirection success
baselines_compared: 'No Defense' condition (same attacker setup, Mantis absent) is the primary baseline showing the reduction in attacker success
Comparison across three attacker agents (PentestGPTAuto, AutoPenAgent, HackingBuddyGPT) and multiple backend LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4, Claude 3.5 Haiku)
Ablation across decoy type (FTP vs Web-app) and sabotage objective (agent-counterstrike vs agent-tarpit)
No competing DEFENSE method is compared against, as Mantis is presented as a novel first-of-its-kind proactive defense
